content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What’s a movie that actually changed your life or mindset

**Score:** 122
**Body:** Going through a phase where I feel stuck and mentally off track.

Not looking for “good movies” ,I mean films that genuinely hit you and make you want to fix your life, change your habits, or see things differently after watching.

Something that lingers in your head for days.

What’s that one movie for you?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was young and close to proposing g when ai first saw Blood Diamond.  Changed my whole perspective on jewelry.
2. I watched What Dreams May Come when I was in a very dark spot mentally, the kind of state a lot of people unfortunately don’t come back from, and watching that movie literally made me value life a lot more and my outlook spirituality, ironically The Fisher King as well. Rip Robin 🫶 ur films actually saved me at my absolute worse 🤧
3. 127 hours

If you are going somewhere by yourself, you should have somebody to know it or if you don’t wanna share your whereabouts, just remind someone that if you don’t show up in a period of time, you are either lost or dead
4. Fight club. The things you own, end up owning you.
5. Good Will Hunting  - It’s never late to get yourself and your life together, get up chin up and go chase your dreams , work on it with all your efforts. 

Also, about friendship, true friends can heal you to the core and can bring hell lot of improvements. Cherish it!
6. "Thank You For Smoking" with Aaron Eckhart and cameo from J.K. Simmons

Dynamic and broken family life trying to make it work, morally-questionable expert lobbyist who truly loves his son. The "Ice Cream" scene sticks with me to this day, I've done a fair amount of debates with that mindset.
7. Into The Wild was that movie for me for a while
8. Dead Poets Society. Blew my mind as a teenager when I first saw it. The idea that life should be lived deliberately and deeply and, in the words of Thoreau, "not when we come to die discover that we had not lived."
9. I just watched Requiem for a Dream this morning and my goodness, the nervous energy I had to be productive and do something with my life felt almost urgent by the end of the film.
10. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty - the importance of enjoying the moments and actually living life

## 2. Movies that look like fluffy romcoms but cover other issues

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I'm looking for movies that resemble romcoms but address other topics in addition to the romance. 

It would be great if they're still somehow fun, humorous, or sweet. There can be some tough elements, but I don't want to be depressed afterward.

Some examples:

Catch and Release - betrayal, grief

Confessions of a Shopaholic - shopping compulsion

In Her Shoes - family estrangement, mental health, self-esteem

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Silver Linings Playbook 2012
2. Jerry Maguire - identity and fulfillment. Living meaningfully. Jerry starts out the cutthroat agent and realizes not only does that not make him happy, nothing about his life is what he wants or how he wants to do it.

500 Days of Summer - it looks like a romcom, but it is not. It's really about the end of a relationship not the start, and it's more about coming to terms with the idea that you can't force a relationship.
3. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
4. About Time is a great one for this - marketed as a romcom with the time travel gimmick but the emotional core is actually about the father-son relationship. caught me off guard the first time.
5. Crazy Rich Asians, The Big Sick.
6. Shirley Valentine 
7. Prime 

He’s Just Not That Into You
8. Just watched Voicemails for Isabelle and cried/sobbed through most of it but it was also hilarious and really good!
9. Very interesting request - love it! You've got some great suggestions already, but maybe I can add:

Moonstruck (1987) - slice of life family dynamics

Waitress (2007) - escaping the doldrums of a disappointing life (and crummy husband)
10. the "After" series on Netflix!

## 3. I’m in the mood for something eerie, small town vibes, atmospheric in a breezy fall season way. Those gloomy, good witch hallmark type mysteries but creepier

**Score:** 13
**Body:** I’m not sure if I’m even explaining this right 😅. It doesn’t even have to have witches. I want like the hallmark small town during fall vibes but really eerie/creepy plot. Does that exist? I know practical magic might be first to come to mind but I’ve seen it like 15 times and once recently so not that pls lol. TYIA!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Something Wicked This Way Comes* (1983)
2. Not a movie but a show Widows Bay. 
3. Needful Things (1993)
4. The Fog
5. The Gift (2000)
6. The Witches of Eastwick 
7. oh this is such a specific mood and i love it. Over the Garden Wall is the exact thing — autumn small-town eerie, gorgeous and gentle but genuinely unsettling underneath. it’s a miniseries so easy to binge in one cozy night

for movies, The Autopsy of Jane Doe nails the creepy small-town atmosphere, and Gretel & Hansel if you want something witchy and visually gloomy but not hallmark-corny. The Wicker Man (1973) too if you want the eerie folk-horror version of that small community vibe
8. Widows Bay first season just finished.  
It was a slow start but that’s fits the New England island vibe. 
9. Not a movie but the limited Netflix series Midnight Mass
10. Caddo Lake (2024)  
Wolf Man (2025)  
The Legend of Ochi (2025)

## 4. Movies like Misery (1990)

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I’m looking for some good recommendations which are very similar to the movie, like someone is help captive with a good plot and not random slasher gore movie! Thanks alot

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Send Help (2026) gave me Misery vibes
2. The loved Ones (2009) 
3. Gerald's Game
4. What ever happened to Baby Jane
5. 10 Cloverfield Lane
6. Save the Green Planet! and its remake, Bugonia. Between the two I enjoyed the original the most, but both are fine.
7. Hard pivot on the "captivity but not gore" brief: Room (2015, Brie Larson won Best Actress, the captivity is the setup and the second half is even more devastating), 10 Cloverfield Lane (John Goodman as a doomsday-prepper captor who may or may not be telling the truth about why you can't leave, the genre never goes where you expect), Berlin Syndrome (Australian woman backpacks to Berlin, meets a charming local, can't get out of his apartment, slow psychological dread), and The Disappearance of Alice Creed (UK indie, two kidnappers, one captive, the entire film is three people in a flat and the twists keep coming). All four hit the Misery vibe without leaning on gore.
8. 'Calvaire' (2004)
9. Sorry, wrong number (1948)
10. Pet (2016)

## 5. Movies that highlight Hong Kong as a city

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I just got back from a trip to Hong Kong and absolutely loved the vibes of the city and the strong urban feel of it.  
I’m looking for movies that highlight Hong Kong and immerse you in it! I know the city has changed a lot over the years but movies from any era would be great!

Thank you in advance!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wong to the Kar to the Wai.
  - This is the answer

Everytime I think of HK, I just think of Chungking Express straight away, especially the Central - Mid-Level escalators 
2. The Dark Knight
3. John Woo’s Hong Kong action films with Chow Yun-fat for sure
4. Bloodsport
5. Any Jackie Chan film from the 80s and 90s. 
6. Police Story 1 & 2 are both fun Jackie Chan martial arts movies that show off different parts of the city back in the 80s when the movies were made.

Chungking Express is a gorgeous film set in Hong Kong! In the Mood for Love is set there too but shows off the city a bit less.
7. Forced Vengeance (Chuck Norris movie)

\[Keep it mind, it's Chuck Norris—so you can expect the story to feel like a karate expert wrote it instead of a screenwriter.\]
8. Forget the name but i think the movie The Departed was based off of.
  - Infernal Affairs

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Disney looking at the Toy Story 5 reviews like:

**Score:** 1481

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Till we’re 90” and then they’ll just use your AI voice for 100 more movies after that
  - Yeah expect the Tim Allen and Tom Hanks estates to lease the voice rights for 10m +2% ownership per project for future installments. 

I know I’d put it in my will if I were them. 
  - Yeah but they’ll get a residual off the AI voice probably, or at least Tom Hank’s brother will.
2. That series ended with 3. Everything since is nothing more than Disney greed
  - I have bad news for you about the first 3, they were also about Disney greed. 
    - Technically speaking, the first two were Pixar alone as an independent studio. Toy Story 3 came out 4 years after Disney acquired the company from Steve Jobs.
      - Steve Jobs owned Pixar at one point? Huh
        - "Prior to Disney's acquisition, Pixar was an independent company majority-owned by Apple co-founder Steve Jobs, who purchased the studio from George Lucas (then part of Lucasfilm) in 1986"
  - 4 and 5 were pretty entertaining
    - The original trilogy (the Andy trilogy if you would) are a solid trilogy that tells a great story. 4 and 5 (the Bonnie era) are really about character pieces told through the toys. 4 dealt with Woody and moving on, while 5 focuses on Jesse and acceptance and growing up. While no one can deny the greatness of the first 3, the last 2 have definitely proven they have something to say and aren't just cashgrabs. 

## 2. What is the most gruesome torture scene you’ve ever seen?

**Score:** 867
**Body:** What a movie torture scene that’s so gruesome and hard to watch, you’ve never forgotten it?

For me it’s the knotted rope torture in Casino Royale. To say I cringe in this scene is an understatement.

The sound effects of the impact and Craig’s reactions are so visceral, it feels way too real.  
Amazing movie, but this scene is a tough watch.

What’s that scene for you?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. All of Hostel 
  - Yeah the blow torch to the eye. 
2. Reservoir dogs  
![gif](giphy|xFnyGXffad2jQAFR1y)
  - That great song will never be the same for me
  - https://i.redd.it/7686kwsisi8h1.gif
3. the ankle breaking scene in Misery is the worst for me.  I cannot watch that movie ever again. 
4. Bone Tomahawk probably. Or Poughkeepsie Tapes. 
5. The intestine crank scene in The Cell stuck with me for sure
6. The Cell. Vince Vaughn guts 
7. Ever seen the original Martyrs?

## 3. What is your favorite Jack Black performance?

**Score:** 392

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|VHRtUP0qp781a)
  - "She said I'm gonna burn this mother down.. and I was like you better not!"
2. Saving Silverman
3. School of Rock
  - Easily. School of Rock defines Jack Black to me.
4. Bernie 
5. ![gif](giphy|yCGI5RMNvp5i8)
6. ![gif](giphy|oOxBRrNw3PJo7ixeBh)
7. ![gif](giphy|umXZ6MNman8TC)
8. https://preview.redd.it/75yttw3wrg8h1.jpeg?width=1280&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=800bc92c71b976347b8b2af65d6fe07b803f1cc1

Not even close

## 4. Remembering James Tolkien on his 95th birthday. What’s your favorite role from his filmography?

**Score:** 311
**Body:** I’ll start. Stinger in Top Gun and Mr. Strickland in Back To The Future movies

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. SLACKER!
  - mcfly your shirt your shoes get out
  - Did that guy ever have hair???
2. You screw up just this much you’ll be flying a cargo plane full of rubber dogshit out of Hong Kong!!
  - "Good Luck Gentlemen" 
3. \* Tolkan 
4. Stinger: Son, your ego is writing checks your body can't cash. You've been busted, you lost your qualifications as section leader three times, put in hack twice by me, with a history of high speed passes over five air control towers, and one admiral's daughter!

Goose: Penny Benjamin?

Maverick: We're saving her for the sequel, not that you'll be around for it.
5. Eat lead slackers!
6. There was more then one role?  I feel like after he got out of the navy he became a small town sheriff the retired to Eternia 
7. Loved his book trilogy. Fav character was Jim Bombadil.

## 5. Happy birthdays to John Goodman and Nicole Kidman! John is 74 years old and Nicole is now 59 years old. What are your favorite characters they’ve played?

**Score:** 207
**Body:** I’ll start. For John, it’s Sully in Monsters Inc. and Pacha in Emperor’s New Groove. For Nicole, it’s Dr. Claire Lewicki in Days of Thunder, Alice Hartford in Eyes Wide Shut, and Dr. Chase Meridian in Batman Forever. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|13t2OTCFzCqJbO)

2. I thought Goodman was amazing in 10 Cloverfield lane
3. And yeah, Nicole Kidman was my first childhood crush. My favorite role of her is The Others (2001). She absolutely nailed it

6. I’m absolutely fascinated with Nicole’s role in The Others

8. Kidman:
The Hours
The Others
To Die For

Goodman:
Big Lebowski
O,  Brother, Where Art Thou
Monsters, Inc.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Nymphomaniac vol I & 2 - profound, extreme filmmaking...and not what I was expecting

**Score:** 112
**Body:** This film(s) really is something I'd put off seeing for some time. I was never a Lars von Trier fan (could completely feel his inventive brilliance in Antichrist and Melancholia, but neither film left anything in me, almost entirely forgetting them other than the feeling of unsettledness they gave). I honestly didn't want to watch 5 hours of f&cking as some sort of cinematic experiment, but a recent review on TrueFilm and comments there convinced me somewhat spontaneously to order the BluRay. The film wasn't what I had previsioned. First of all, vol 1 is really a beautiful, enaging film, and though it is somewhat about "addiction" (in this case sex addiction, but could standing in for all), its a much more human film that really felt like it was also about all the things that lead to addictive "release" or shelter in pleasure, which gave it both an incredible specificity (this character, this person), but also a universal humanity. Not just in addicts, but in all of us, in how we use and relate to any pleasure. The conversation that drives the retelling of this woman's life is just beautifully shot in a spare, "monk like" room and serves as an expertly tempo'd weave into and out of the stories of a developing psychology. But, afoot in all of this are some very profound ideas that go well beyond simply the story of a psychology. Once we get to the theme of the Angler we start to feel that there are some VERY big philosophical stakes...at stake, bringing pressure onto the very foundations of our sense of morality, Good vs Evil, passing through Christianity and probably back to Plato. Seligman becomes a very interesting compound figure in this...and reference to Plato's use of the example of the "angler" in the dialogue The Sophist, can be no accident. (The "angler" is evocative of the sophist - not a seeker of truth, but somewhat of an intellectual conman, which can map onto later Christianity's "deceiver" Devil mythos - who fishes for the youth, and teaches them with sophistry/falseness.) As Seligman describes how the older, bigger fish seclude themselves in the well protected nooks of a river, very hard to catch in solitude in solitude, we realize at some point he is really describing himself. And the fishing fly, the false bait, may very well be "Joe", who may become over time a very, very subtle seductress, daring him to "bite"...a "hook-er". >!He also may stand in for an intellectualizing film critic (who doesn't really feel a film directly), a theologically absolving confession priest, or a theorizing therapist (who doesn't actually "hear" his patient), or as I chose to see him eventually, something of Wim Wenders-like angel, who understands human beings at a remove. He is a completely asexual, intellect-oriented being, listening to a overly sexualized person, who is trying to convince him of her (evil?) sinful, awful character. The film reads at some level as almost a At The Gates of Heaven weighing of a soul, as Joe's life story unfolds, and the way von Trier braids all these levels together, from Plato to the grittiest part of human experience is nothing short of spectacular. Volume 1 kind of blew me away. !<

>!Volume 2 was a different story. I was really looking forward to it - in part because it had Dafoe and Goth, two of my favorite actors - but it veered in a much less satisfying direction. It's his film, and his vision, so I don't wish he did anything differently, but the spell of the first volume was broken. A large measure of this is because he took on various tropes of extreme sexuality (porn tropes like 2 black guys on a white girl, sado-masochism, gangbang), along with political tropes of debates over sexuality (an absolute brave but incredibly hard to watch abortion scene, meant to take on the bodily "reality" beneath these debates), that made the film much, much less unified. Perhaps some of this because part of the problem with "porn" is that it breaks the cinematic spell. As the philosopher Zizek once said (paraphrasing) "when you see porn you suddenly feel that the film exists just to show "this", film becomes a prop holding it up". This was not the effect IN the film, when taking on cliches and tropes of porn, in that the scenes felt very un-erotic, as least for me, they often felt clinical, as if dissecting the human sexual condition. But the breaking of the cinematic spell, in borrowing from porn, or casting scenes full of cliche, did happen (for me). This distancing, which may very well have been von Trier's philosophical goal. He's trying confront taboo, to strip down the human condition, but taboo also structures our eyes and how narraative is processed, so it made it also feel UNREAL (which I suspect was not his aim). By the time the film got to Joe joining the "other army" (in this case it felt like Dafoe was the Devil's stand in) and getting some sort of incoherent "collection" job, the story itself felt like it was falling apart for me. I had little narrative investment in her somehow moving to the Devil's side (if only allegorically, or metaphorically) and exacting a provocative "revenge" or flipping the script on men. Not only was the story not believable, the performances really were not as well (despite Gainsbourg being off the charts good elsewise). Even Dafoe and Goth were uncomfortably off-the-mark, and I felt like I had really entered into a film only of Ideas. Trying on ideas. Making points. Maybe some of this was intentional. It's possible, but the transgressive, graphic sex set-ups and aesthetics, the unrealistic story turns just made the film MUCH less enjoyable and interesting to watch (though the political, psychological, philosophical debates between Seligman and Joe remained strong). !<

>!The ending was absolutely fantastic. Yes, indeed, she was a "lure" of a kind, seducing the fallen or in-between angel, perhaps very much against her own will, positioning the dangers of sexuality put onto women in society, something the film suggests may even be connected to how the Intellect & the Body is divided by socialized gender. She was either an accidental, or very very subtle...or ideologically imposed "whore of Babylon" who could seduce even the most asexual being, and she perfectly gets her revenge, remembering to rack the pistol. !<

In the end the film is one that I'm very glad that I watched (thank you to True Film commenters who lead to me do so. I'd probably put it in the same category as Noe's Irreversible which was an incredible masterpiece, but maybe not a film I'd watch again. This film...I "might" watch again.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Excellent write up. I watched these movies a bunch because I once met a guy who swore they were a “masterwork”, and, man, how he would talk about them, and I kept trying to see what he saw, but you share a lot of my own thoughts on the two films. Vol 1 nearly gets there at times. In the end the whole thing simply does not coalesce for me. As you say, the spell breaks. There are moments I think back on, for sure… it’s brave filmmaking, and I do miss Von Trier’s ballsy filmmaking. I would also be interested what you think of The House That Jack Built. Again, nice write up.
  - I'm kind of interested in the idea that just trying to break taboo-ness itself, by clinically (or quotationally, in terms of porn cliches) showing is maybe fundamentally impossible. That when you are "showing" a taboo thing, the cinema effect just collapses. If we allow the idea that Joe is expressing some of von Trier's aims in her own dialogue, the film is trying to strip taboo away, and that may be logically impossible...in this kind of approach. It's not how cinema works. He's breaking cinema, in a way.

If so, profoundly - and perhaps the film itself has this argument within it - without the scaffolding of romantic interest, or of erotized lustfulness, things that cinema history is filled with and expert at generating, you simply can't present "sex", without it feeling UNREAL. It has to exist instead in a generated fantasy space (again, cinema's forte) to **feel** real. If so, some of the film's failure, the breaking of the spell, in volume 2 actually proves its philosophical point...which would be kind of insane.

But...it wouldn't explain how some of the story turns in vol 2 became so unreal and almost flat, or acted in an off-kilter way...but, maybe, just maybe, this is von Trier leaving the realm of the "human" and entering into flat archetypes/tropes...almost with a node to Plato's realm of Ideas. If so, that forces me to only, or mostly, enjoy this film as a "Seligman".
  - The House that Jack Built is a masterpiece in filmmaking. But first, my thoughts on Nymphomaniac. 

I watched Nymphomaniac last week because it popped up on my Prime recommendations, which surprised me because I had thought of this movie as pseudo-pornography dressed up as film from “watching it” in my early days (skimming to the fun parts, a slave to my cunt). To say I had a low preconceived notion of the quality of this film set, would be a gross understatement. I fully expected to get ten minutes in and turn it off in disgust because of how abhorrently bad it was.

How very wrong and foolish of me.   
Obviously, I was unfamiliar with Lars Von Trier. I had literally no idea this man existed. I am nowhere near what you would call a film buff, but I am not a virgin to film. Funny how when you say that out loud, it connotes the same sentiment. “I am not aversion to film”.

In broken English, yes, but perhaps really the most pure form of the language. 

“No can do,” “Long time no see,” “Why use lot word when few do trick?” 

As Joe says in part two, essentially, perhaps we should say things we mean, without the burden of having to constantly dance around the line of political correctness. I would not take it as far as she does with “negro” for the record, but my cunt is my cunt, is my cunt is my cunt. I am a woman. And I have a cunt.

What a hypocrite I am here, though, going on an on in effusive literary mode when I had just extolled the virtues of simplicity. “Saying right but doing wrong,” to quote her again. 

Yes I can do that — just pull perfectly placed quotes out of the air ad nauseam, until your jaw is on the fucking floor, and my literary cock in place of where your tongue used to be. 

But I digress, as Seligman so often does to immense perfection in the film. Seriously, the dialogue and stories, the little tidbits he shares — are what hooked me to the film immediately. Again, fitting how the fly fishing comparison he makes is the debut of these digressions (that’s French for “beginning”).

I understand that the character is being mocked by the director as hypocritical intelligentsia, but I love his character nonetheless. Skarsgard plays him perfectly. The voice fits so well. Both of their voices are beautiful. They sound like angels, and that is really a key element to the film. They talk so much, that the voices had to sound spectacular for it to have any chance of being spectacular. Is the film spectacular? No.

But I have digressed again. Back to me not being a virgin to film. 

I have watched many movies, but at some point, I lost my taste for them and switched primarily to television. This was a great choice honestly, and I know that may cause some of you to recoil in disgust, given the subreddit I am currently giving the gift of my words to, but the enjoyment one can extract from hundreds of hours of the brilliance of  The Wire, Breaking Bad, Better Call Saul, Ozark, Vice Principals (all 10/10 — and I have a very high fucking standard of what a 10 is. Prime Brad Pitt is an 8, tops)… dwarfs what you can get out of a single film, no matter how good. Simply by virtue of length and sheer volume of content. You know I’m right. Say it bitch, fucking say it. 

Season 1 of either Fargo or True Detective, to me, are superior to the entirety of Tarantino’s work. And he is my favorite director. Inglorious Basterds is a masterpiece, and what I consider to be the absolute pinnacle of film. A common choice, maybe a common refrain, but it is correct. It’s simply the best. I cannot be dissuaded of that.

Perhaps that gives you enough insight into my tastes to continue properly. If you have not gleaned that I am of very high intellect and brain power, then get fucking lost. I value silver over gold, yet I appreciate good gold as much as I appreciate anything else deemed art, by ceremony, or by my cerebellum.

However, I must say that watching Nymphomaniac reignited my passion for film. I had been watching seven television series concurrently before I watched Nympho, and have been doing so for years, without sabbatical. Not always 7, sometimes 10 or more even (it takes a lot of artistic media food to fuel the voracious appetite of my increasingly powerful brain.) I have not watched a single minute of television since. Instead, I have been watching movies. I feel like one of those pornographic actresses who “takes a big black cock for the first time.”

I realize now that I should make this a separate post. More people deserve to read the magic that is my words, come to life on a black mirror. But going on, to conclude my prelude of Nymphomaniac: my thoughts, I’ll skip to the end, saving that for my own post on this forum — and you can have the privilege of getting to anticipate that moment, when it comes. If it comes.

Nymphomaniac is a good movie.

Now, to The House that Jack Built. Like I said, a masterpiece in filmmaking. 

Does it eclipse the greatness of Inglorious Basterds? No.

The rest of that will be in my own post. I’m a bit of a sadist. A tease. A “Lick the head and go to bed” type of girl. Yes, I did just make that up. Yes, if I ever let any of you greasy fucks cum in me, Jesus would return to us in 9 months — no matter how inferior the Y. Again, on the spot. Off the top. I impress myself sometimes. You’re welcome, and God bless me and my beautiful mind.
2. I watched it all together so I didnt feel a significant change between the two volumes. I mean, she changed when the actress changed and her behavior became more overtly depressing for her, but it seemed right the right direction. I think the sex tropes make sense since she needed to do more extreme things so these are obvious options she can try, what else can she think of that isnt a porn cliche? Her whole sex life is basically porn, its not like she is guided by any specific personal desire focused on a person or an act, so she's just doing everything. 

The ending really hit me like those purposefully long jokes that go into random details just to hit you with a simple punchline. I still dont know what to think about it, whether I like it or not, but I appreciate the shock effect. For me it was completely unexpected and it effectively had a shock factor very few things do, and that says something after 5 h of that. 

I am glad in a sense that Seligman didnt end up being just a removed asexual benign presence who is just there to listen and has no personal needs. From his side, I liked the development and it made sense. But im not sure what to think of her reaction and what it means. A condemnation of him would just be too stupid after everything else. I could see it psychologically though, as if she finally connected to someone intellectually and one time in her life didnt want it to be about sex, whereas for him it was seemingly the opposite, he observes everything intellectually and this was the time he wanted to experience sex. In the end despite building this honest bond, both characters profoundly disappoint each other. In that sense, the ending is really cruel. That's not a bad thing. 

Overall a great movie, for me one of the most shocking and brutal endings of all time, not sure what the director's message was with it but it resonated with me for a long time after I watched it. 

This and Melancholia are my favorite movies of his, though the house that Jack built was great and I appreciated Idioterne. Antichrist was very good too. But out of everything, it definitely fucked me up the most in those few final minutes.
  - I like all your thoughts here, and seriously appreciate you writing them out. Someone in comments on a recent, previous thread suggested that I watch the film with a break between the two volumes, so I watched them on back to back nights. I'm very glad I did as it really let vol 1 sink into me, but, on the other hand, it may have produced a sense of disjunction (which you didn't feel). I like looking at it through your eyes though. 

To me the ending is incredible. As mentioned, I think von Trier is making this incredible point where women are positioned, eternally, between their own pleasure and being "the whore", the seductress, the lure. And there is no way out of this. 
    - That explanation of the ending isnt really resonating with me. Seems like its besides the point, or too boring.
I definitely think his character is no worse than hers. They both look at the other person in the end as a source of a need they never had fulfilled, and the result for both is ultimately unsatisfactory (though he gets killed for it.) But I guess the movie is about the inability to fulfil any desire anyway, so even when you completely shift it to another realm, it still only ends in disappointment


Btw I didnt watch it all in one go but I didnt pay attention to it being in 2 parts.

Nothing like I thought would be."...and then in vol 2 I was like "Ah! This. Okay. Several scenes I just forced myself through, or half cringed at...and really in the end only the ideas of the film, circling back, justifying it all, made it worth having watched. So, I kinda was (very) wrong about the film, but I also had been somewhat right too. It has that thing where he is putting a stick in your eye and saying "See, life has lots of sticks in the eye"...and now let's philosophize about it. For me, vol 1 really acts as a kind of salve which helps vol 2. Though vol 1 has some hard parts in it. Also, the things in vol 2 which have that kind of cringe or taboo breaking, they didn't really play like I had feared. I just thought there was going to be a lot of "pounding" (to be blunt about it), and it was not really that. It was kind of more visual, situational, set up, that drew on cliches and tropes, and the clinicalness of things.

Some of my wariness towards the film was that I thought I was going to be bored by the film and all the "pounding", which I would have been, but the human story, and the ideas that are at stake, and that it isn't just endless porno setups, made it so that wasn't really the case. I was engaged throughout, and the ending is very satisfying. It feels rewarding to have made it to that point.

4. I enjoyed vol. 1, and some of vol. 2, but I hated the ending of that movie so much it really soured me on the rest of it. Skarsgård's whole character was written with a truely awful understanding on asexuality, so much so that even before his final act it's hard to find his character beleivebale at all. Asexuality is just used as something to mark the character as "pure", seemingly uncorruptable, and as if not experience the human condition, and it's really not much different that depicting a gay character as a child predator becaose of their sexuality. I understand what he was going for, but what he was going for doesn't work. 
 
I did also find that it was a bit of a case of the messge and the medium not really working together very well. Despite everything in the movie, it does really pander to the male gaze in way that I don't think benefits the it at all. Lars von Trier's work is just lacking something major for me. Maybe part of it is that I stongly disagree with a lot of what his films are trying to say a lot of the time, but I can't help but compre Nymphomanic to Catherine Breillat' Anatomy of Hell, which is a far superior film. It is more explicit than anything in either volume of Nymphomanic, but it is much more purpose driven and very much weponizes the male gaze in a way that I don't think Lars von Trier is capible of doing.

## 2. Cure (Kiyoshi Kurosawa, 1997) scene composition

**Score:** 55
**Body:** I just watched Kiyoshi Kurosawa’s Cure for the first time, and man what an amazing film 

When I saw the trailer the other day what really stuck out to me was just how good it looked, like it really gives meaning to the phrase “every frame a painting”- every scene looked so damn good.

I was not disappointed at all upon watching it. 

I don’t really know many technical terms but it was the way the scenes were framed, lit, blocked and the composition- like how things sat in the frame. It just looks so great.

One thing that stuck out to me was how the camera rarely moved. All of the movement occurs within the frame. It was as if Kurosawa just set the camera up and let the actors go to work. 

Theres some great camera work in the scenes as well, like in the scene where they interrogate the police officer who killed his coworker- the way the camera just sits in the middle of the frame and then finally moves to the chair in the bottom left, up to the top left, following the police officer up to the top right as he acts out his hypnotism with what appears to be a coffee stirrer and then moves back to the middle table.

The way the characters moved in the scene, and the camera eventually moved with them, and the way the fit in the frame, was brilliant.

The movie was chock full of brilliant moments like this.

I’d Like to know more about this, because I don’t really have any frame of reference for it.

I’d also lIke to know of more directors/films that use this approach. I do think both Ozu and Akira Kurosawa use this approach, but it’s something that’s sorely missing in Hollywood films, where cameras often cut back and forth between people in dialogue, and it just seems rare to set up a scene for people to move in, as opposed to moving around in the scene in a way that just feels different (imo it doesn’t make what’s going on visually very interesting)

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorite movies. It is amazing all around, but I think my favorite thing is just how it feels aesthetically. It is generally a dark and uncomfortable atmosphere, but it feels so good to me. Masterpiece.
2. If you appreciate great framing and static camera placements, I’d recommend anything by Jacques Tati(especially Playtime), anything by Chantal Akerman, or First Reformed for a more recent film that does it perfectly.
3. Jonathan Glazer's The Zone of Interest (2023) has a lot of still shots where the camera is still and the characters move and interact in the space.

And Barry Lyndon (1975) by Stanley Kubrick is incredible if you haven't seen that. So many scenes are like gorgeous still paintings, and shot with natural light.

Roma (2018) by Alfonso Caurón has a more dynamic camera, but it moves fluidly and immerses the viewer in the world of the story, while often casually framing shots beautifully, in my opnion.



4. Possibly my favorite aspect of the Cure’s cinematography is the way Kurosawa frames many of Takabe’a conversations with Mamiya. Frequently, the camera is locked on Takabe, center framed, and Mamiya is obscured or otherwise left offscreen. As Mamiya speaks to Takabe, unseen but palpably present, it is as if he is a voice in Takabe’s head, a perfect illustration of the severity of his hold on his psyche and his goal of prodding Takabe into acquiescing to his dark impulses.

## 3. Tokyo Godfather Movie Review

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Tokyo Godfathers was absolutely perfect, in my opinion, it is one of the best Christmas movies ever. It’s such a gripping story packed into just an hour and a half, filled with so many twists and turns. All the characters in the movie were so well written, and the main trio was incredibly likeable.

&#x200B;

​I was especially impressed by the depth given to the gay character, I didn't expect that at all, because in most movies, they are either portrayed as a joke or made completely unlikeable, but that wasn't the case here at all. The pacing was excellent, the director didn't waste a single second. The animation was also so fluid and high quality, and I really loved how the colors and shadows were used throughout the film. Every single scene contributed to the story and character development. The movie was heartwarming, funny, and emotional. For me, it was a solid 10/10.

## 4. Discussion on Young and Beautiful/Jeune et Jolie

**Score:** 13
**Body:** I watched this film a while ago and found it very fascinating (and relatable) but I couldn’t find many people discussing the main character’s psyche online. 

My personal view of WHY she does what she does, as a young woman (22) as well, is a mixture of self destruction, daddy issues, and “Lolita-complex” (for lack of better words?) let me try my best to explain what I mean. 

I have these tendencies myself too (although I am not nor have I ever been a prostitute). I think she is firstly depressed and part of her depression is manifesting in self destructive tendencies. Also, clearly her father is absent and she has a strained relationship with her stepfather, so there are the daddy issues. And then what I mean by “Lolita-complex” can be very well translated by the Mitski song “liquid smooth” where she says: 

*I'm beautiful, I know 'cause it's the season*  
*But what am I to do with all this beauty?*  
*Biology, I am an organism*  
*I'm chemical, that's all, that is all*  
*I'm liquid smooth, come touch me too*  
*And feel my skin is plump and full of life*  
*I'm in my prime*  
*I'm liquid smooth, come touch me too*  
*I'm at my highest peak, I'm ripe*  
*About to fall, capture me*

Basically, I think a lot of young and beautiful girls feel like they are expiring. I feel this a lot. Each birthday feels like I’m aging out of my prime, like I’m closer and closer to hitting that invisible wall that women do when they’re no longer young. (Sad but that’s how society is. Let’s not argue about that here, that’s not the point.) So she feels a desperate need to “take advantage” of her youth and beauty right now before it’s gone, which is manifesting in her prostituting herself to older men. 

I tried my best to word my thoughts here! I hope it’s understandable. Would love to hear other people’s input and if anyone could word it better than me. LOL

So by definition this would be a blockbuster even if it didn’t cost millions to make it.

Thanks! 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are two ways people use the term "blockbuster", one is based purely on metrics. So a movie that makes a ton of money. The second, which I think is the more common one now, is a movie made by a major studio with the specific intention to make a lot of money. 

A lot of people have problems with the second because it's more commerce driven that art driven.

  - The first definition is originally correct whereas the second is really what studios call ‘tentpole releases’, in other words a film intended to bring in the cash to make the quarterlies look good.

Personally I enjoy films across the spectrum, from Slow cinema through to successful Hollywood studio features but I don’t think it’s cynical to say that there’s a lot of films, especially franchise movies, that are made not to support or continue a good or necessary story (let alone be particularly well made or interesting to look at) but simply because they will make money and studios have board members.
2. A blockbuster is a film that has been incredibly successful financially.  However, some films are made as an artistic statement based on a story an artist just had to tell.  Then that film comes out at just the right time and just hits a nerve with society at large.   The Godfather, Jaws, Rocky, etc...

Then some films are made for the sole purpose of making money.  Decisions are made based on a commercial viability as opposed to an artistic one.  Like say, the right actor for the part is not a big enough star so that person won't get cast in favor of an inferior performer that will draw a bigger crowd.

It's not that movies that succeed are inferior, it's that often times, movies made for the sole purpose of capitalizing on a trend just tend to be inferior because they are created under nefarious circumstances. 
3. The most popular media is usually lowest common denominator stuff that has the broadest appeal to the widest base. It’s usually sort of unchallenging, derivative, standard stuff rather than anything novel or unique. Then occasionally some unique vision will become popular and financially successful because people are starved for novelty. It’s just the messy intersection of business and art. If something is successful the suits in charge will do the same thing again and again until they’ve driven it into the ground and everyone is sick of it.
4. Blockbuster films are already very popular by virtue of their definition. 

In order to be globally successful, any ideas that might be challenging or offensive to conservative markets or unsophisticated or uneducated audience members, including children, need to be removed or downplayed.

Products made with this intention are usually quite generic, and offer little substantive value other than lowest common denominator entertainment.

McDonalds is probably the best selling burger in the world.

I don't believe their bottom line is affected by what people have to say about it, and I doubt its reputation needs defending.

Let’s say for example: Obsession, this film was made with a very low budget but is now one of the highest grossing films with over 200 million dollars worldwide. So by definition this would be a blockbuster even if it didn’t cost millions to make it.
